patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
Re: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch updates the NFS (Network File System) code to ensure that inode ctime is updated after a removexattr operation, which must be done synchronously due to potential delayed error conditions and lack of ability to update only the ctime in delegated timestamp updates. This change aligns with existing guidance for operations like setxattr() and removexattr(), where synchronous execution is required to handle possible failures. The patch addresses a specific issue related to NFS delegation and timestamp updates, ensuring that inode metadata remains accurate even when using delegations.

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the current formulation of 'operations that can report delayed error' is too broad, as any NFSv4 operation may encounter a temporary condition and return an error at execution time. He suggested rephrasing the guidance to focus on whether the client can complete the syscall locally and defer authoritative execution without violating its semantics.
review_comments.raw_body:
One concern I have with the "ops that can report delayed error"
formulation is that, in practice, any NFSv4 operation may encounter
a temporary condition and return NFS4ERR_DELAY at execution time.
Even a simple operation like GETFH may need to allocate reply
resources and fail transiently. As such, the ability to convey delay
is not a distinguishing property of a particular operation.

I think the relevant distinction is instead whether the client may
complete the syscall locally and defer authoritative execution
and/or result until a later point without violating its semantics.
Some operations fit that model, but others do not.

Even WRITE is not inherently in the "may be deferred" category;
that depends on the data path. For example, in a pNFS configuration
where WRITE is routed synchronously to an MDS, completion semantics
differ from a buffered writeback model. This suggests that the
classification should not be tied to specific opcodes, but to the
completion semantics of the operation in the context in which it
is executed.

Given that, it may be more useful for the spec to describe delegated
timestamp behavior in terms of these semantics:

   o Operations for which the client may lawfully acknowledge
   completion before authoritative server execution has completed
   may use delegated timestamp authority, subject to the constraints
   of that deferred-completion model.

   o Operations that require authoritative execution status before
   completion of the user-visible request must be executed synchronously
   and must not rely on deferred timestamp resolution.

This avoids per-op enumeration and allows future operations to be
classified based on their semantics rather than requiring explicit
specification updates.
---
Tom Haynes <loghyr@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Thomas Haynes' concern about synchronous operations on delegated timestamp updates, explaining that disabling c/mtime updates when handing out delegations and clamping the delegated timestamp update to between a recorded time and 'now' ensures that nothing rolls backward while respecting client updates.

Author clarified that he meant to describe operations like unstable WRITEs and asynchronous COPY operations, which can fail after the initial operation, not necessarily returning NFS4ERR_DELAY.
review_comments.raw_body:
We basically disable c/mtime updates on the delegation when it's handed
out and record the current time (A). We then clamp the delegated
timestamp update to between A and "now" when the SETATTR is done.
That's enough to ensure that nothing rolls backward but we can still
respect the client's update assuming the clocks are close enough.

---

Yeah, I phrased that poorly. I didn't mean something that would return
NFS4ERR_DELAY, but rather operations like unstable WRITEs and
asynchronous COPY operations (and anything else that falls into that
model) that can fail after the server has returned from the initial
operation.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] exportfs: don't pass struct iattr to ->commit_blocks
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch modifies exportfs to not pass struct iattr to ->commit_blocks, potentially simplifying the code.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Noted that the first patch is redundant if this one is applied, suggesting it can be removed.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that the big fat comment is still present, suggesting it's unclear what behavior this patch is intended to document.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Mar 31, 2026 at 01:33:10PM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > Disagree: I think the change in the first patch needs its own
> > patch description and motivation.
> >
> > > since you're making that change moot (and the
> > > ia_* times don't matter much anyway).
> > > 
> 
> Your call, but note that the changes in the first patch are completely
> removed by this one. There's really no need for that patch if you take
> this one too.

The big fat comment is still left.  The first patch is mostly intended as
clearly documenting what we're doing.  There is no behavior except for
sampling the current time more often in it anyway.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch updates the NFS (Network File System) code to ensure that inode ctime is updated after a removexattr operation, which must be done synchronously due to potential delayed error conditions and lack of ability to update only the ctime in delegated timestamp updates. This change aligns with existing guidance for operations like setxattr() and removexattr(), where synchronous execution is required to handle possible failures. The patch addresses a specific issue related to NFS delegation and timestamp updates, ensuring that inode metadata remains accurate even when using delegations.

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that delegated timestamps cannot guarantee no visible ctime rollback when authoritative timestamp is generated on a different node with a different clock, and suggested either monotonic clamping of exposed ctime or treating change_attr as the serialization signal.

Reviewer Thomas Haynes expressed concern that a per-operation formulation for delegated timestamp behavior may limit extensibility, suggesting instead to define the behavior based on operation properties such as synchronicity and impact on ctime/mtime/atime.
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree we do not want visible ctime rollback, but I do not see how
that can be guaranteed from delegated timestamps alone when the
authoritative timestamp may be generated on a different node with
a different clock and the object may change during the CB_GETATTR
window. That seems to require either monotonic clamping of exposed
ctime, or treating change_attr rather than ctime as the real
serialization signal.

---

One concern I have with a per-op formulation is extensibility. If
delegated timestamp behavior is defined by enumerating specific
operations, then every new operation added to the protocol creates
a fresh ambiguity until the spec is updated again. It seems better
to define the behavior in terms of operation properties - e.g., whether
the operation is synchronously visible, can be deferred/cached at
the client, and whether it affects only ctime versus mtime/atime -
so future operations can be classified without reopening the base
rule.

I.e., I can't tell if you want me to update the spec with
guidance per-op or you are just documenting what you did.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the patch needs to address the issue of synchronous operations like SETXATTR and REMOVEXATTR, which cannot be buffered on the client due to delayed error conditions. The author explained that these operations must continue to be done synchronously even if a delegation is held, citing two reasons: 1) there's no way to update only the ctime in a delegated timestamp update, and 2) these syscalls can fail, so we can't return from them until we know the outcome. The author expressed uncertainty about how to phrase this guidance.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think we probably need some guidance in the spec, and I think that
guidance comes down to: operations that don't have a way to report a
delayed error condition can't be buffered on the client and must
continue to be done synchronously even if a delegation is held.

By way of example: if I do a write() on the client I can buffer that
because userland can eventually do an fsync() to see if it succeeded.
This is not true for syscalls like setxattr() or removexattr(), or most
syscalls that result in a SETATTR operation (chmod(), chown(), etc).

They must be done synchronously because:

1/ there's no way to update only the ctime in a delegated timestamp
update

...and...

2/ these syscalls can fail, so we can't return from them until we know
the outcome.

How best to phrase this guidance, I'm not sure...
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the current formulation of 'operations that can report delayed error' is too broad, as any NFSv4 operation may encounter a temporary condition and return an error at execution time. He suggested rephrasing the guidance to focus on whether the client can complete the syscall locally and defer authoritative execution without violating its semantics.
review_comments.raw_body:
One concern I have with the "ops that can report delayed error"
formulation is that, in practice, any NFSv4 operation may encounter
a temporary condition and return NFS4ERR_DELAY at execution time.
Even a simple operation like GETFH may need to allocate reply
resources and fail transiently. As such, the ability to convey delay
is not a distinguishing property of a particular operation.

I think the relevant distinction is instead whether the client may
complete the syscall locally and defer authoritative execution
and/or result until a later point without violating its semantics.
Some operations fit that model, but others do not.

Even WRITE is not inherently in the "may be deferred" category;
that depends on the data path. For example, in a pNFS configuration
where WRITE is routed synchronously to an MDS, completion semantics
differ from a buffered writeback model. This suggests that the
classification should not be tied to specific opcodes, but to the
completion semantics of the operation in the context in which it
is executed.

Given that, it may be more useful for the spec to describe delegated
timestamp behavior in terms of these semantics:

   o Operations for which the client may lawfully acknowledge
   completion before authoritative server execution has completed
   may use delegated timestamp authority, subject to the constraints
   of that deferred-completion model.

   o Operations that require authoritative execution status before
   completion of the user-visible request must be executed synchronously
   and must not rely on deferred timestamp resolution.

This avoids per-op enumeration and allows future operations to be
classified based on their semantics rather than requiring explicit
specification updates.
---
Tom Haynes <loghyr@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Thomas Haynes' concern about synchronous operations on delegated timestamp updates, explaining that disabling c/mtime updates when handing out delegations and clamping the delegated timestamp update to between a recorded time and 'now' ensures that nothing rolls backward while respecting client updates.

Author clarified that he meant to describe operations like unstable WRITEs and asynchronous COPY operations, which can fail after the initial operation, not necessarily returning NFS4ERR_DELAY.
review_comments.raw_body:
We basically disable c/mtime updates on the delegation when it's handed
out and record the current time (A). We then clamp the delegated
timestamp update to between A and "now" when the SETATTR is done.
That's enough to ensure that nothing rolls backward but we can still
respect the client's update assuming the clocks are close enough.

---

Yeah, I phrased that poorly. I didn't mean something that would return
NFS4ERR_DELAY, but rather operations like unstable WRITEs and
asynchronous COPY operations (and anything else that falls into that
model) that can fail after the server has returned from the initial
operation.

Disagree: I think the change in the first patch needs its own

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1 0/9] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/9] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/9] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation from the io_uring interface. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, decoupling request fetching from the send operation. This simplifies the control flow and reduces unnecessary parameter passing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the io-uring header copying logic in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver by moving it into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(). This makes the code more clear and centralized, allowing for better error handling and rate-limited logging. The function takes three arguments: the user-space ring buffer address, the header to be copied, and its size. It uses the copy_to_user() function to perform the actual copying and returns an error if it fails. The patch modifies several functions in the dev_uring.c file to use this new function instead of duplicating the copying logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 3/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module to make it more clear and centralized. It introduces a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which handles the actual copying of data from user space to kernel space. This change improves error handling and rate-limited logging. The existing code is updated to use this new function instead of directly calling copy_from_user().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 4/9] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when kernel-managed buffer rings are added. The patch adds a new enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types, and modifies two inline functions `copy_header_to_ring` and `copy_header_from_ring` to use this enum type. This change will make the code more maintainable and easier to understand.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 5/9] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state()` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making it more modular and easier to maintain. The existing code is simplified by removing duplicated functionality and replacing it with calls to this new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 6/9] fuse: support buffer copying for kernel addresses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer copying in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, specifically allowing it to work directly on kernel addresses rather than requiring page extraction and kmapping. This is a preparatory step for supporting kernel-managed ring buffers in fuse-over-io-uring. The change involves modifying several functions within the FUSE codebase to handle both user-space and kernel-space buffer copying. The patch also includes some minor cleanups and warnings to ensure correct behavior when working with kernel addresses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 7/9] fuse: add io-uring kernel-managed buffer ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to use a kernel-managed buffer ring for FUSE servers communicating through the io-uring interface, reducing overhead and memory usage. The buffer ring is set on a per-queue basis and can be used incrementally, allowing non-overlapping regions of a buffer to be used simultaneously across multiple requests. This feature requires preregistration of a kernel-managed buffer ring and a memory region large enough to hold the headers before queue creation. The kernel-managed buffer ring will be pinned for the lifetime of the io-ring.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: The reviewer requested a description of the buffer layout be added to the commit message, noting that the current approach is similar to their initial patches but with dynamic configuration and no ioctls.
review_comments.raw_body:
Could you add to the commit message how the buffer layout is? I think
this deserves some explanation.

The appraoch kinds of reminds me to my initial patches where I
configured the ring with an additional ioctl. Until Miklos asked to make
it dynamic and to avoid the ioctls (and config SQEs as well). Basically
here every FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER gets the the queue depth? Wouldn't
make sense to have a setup SQE per queue then? Or one global
CREATE_RINGS config sqe? With the reduced queue series things would be
definitely easier if we would know the number of queues that are
supposed to get created (right now the it has to re-calculate the the
static mapping for every queue).


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed concerns about the layout and mapping of headers in the buffer ring, explaining that they are laid out contiguously in memory and can be mapped by calculating the ent id times sizeof(struct fuse_uring_req_header). They plan to add diagrams for this and will send a v2 patch with updates, including moving the buffer rings and memory accounting infrastructure from io-uring uapi to fuse.

Author Joanne Koong responded to feedback from Bernd Schubert, stating that an additional ioctl or config setup command operation (cmd op) is not needed for the io-uring kernel-managed buffer ring capability in fuse servers. She explained that the registration flow can handle queue depth without a problem and that it simplifies the interface to have all registration sqes pass in the queue depth.

Author asked for clarification on whether the reduced queue series version could use the current registration flow, indicating uncertainty about how to proceed.
review_comments.raw_body:
I will add a visual diagram for this. The headers buffer is laid out
contiguously in memory. On the server side the server is able to map
from ent id to where in the headers buffer the data for that ent
resides by calculating ent_id * sizeof(struct fuse_uring_req_header).
I'll also add a diagram for the buffer ring as well. I am going to be
sending out a v2 that has some updates. The bulk of the fuse logic is
pretty much the same but the buffer rings and memory accounting
infrastructure will be moved to fuse instead of going through the
io-uring uapi.

---

I don't think an additional ioctl or config setup cmd op is needed
because this can go through the registration flow we currently have
without a problem. Every registration sqe will pass the queue_depth in
the fuse_uring_cmd_req data. There is logic to ensure that all ents
for a given queue set the same queue depth. For the code logic, only
the first registration sqe needs to set queue depth, but I think it
makes the interface simpler to just have all the registration sqes
pass in the queue depth, since in the dev_uring code we also account
for edge cases where the same queue gets registered concurrently on
multiple threads. I don't think a separate setup cmd op is necessary
here.

---

Do you have a link to the reduced queue series version you're
referencing? I'm not sure without looking at that code whether that
could use the current registration flow in the same way or whether
that needs a new config sqe.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the code does the same repeated work for every registration submission queue element (reg sqe), suggesting this is counterintuitive and potentially inefficient.

reviewer noted that fuse_uring_get_buf_ring_headers() does not handle the case where the registered memory region is too small to hold the headers, and requested a check for this condition
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah, just a bit counter intuitive to do the same repeated work for
every reg sqe.

---

https://github.com/bsbernd/linux/tree/reduced-nr-ring-queues_4-v7.0

running xfstests now. Let's see if I have access to that when I wake up
;) (I was promised I should have).
If all goes well, I will send the updated series tomorrow.


Thanks,
Bernd

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 8/9] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy data transfer over io-uring for the FUSE file system. It allows privileged servers to preregister a kernel-managed buffer ring, a registered memory region, and a sparse buffer corresponding to the queue depth. This enables the elimination of memory copies between the kernel and userspace for read/write operations, resulting in improved performance. Benchmarks show approximately 20-25% increases in throughput for certain workloads. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to implement this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 9/9] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how to set up kernel-managed buffer rings, which reduce memory allocation requirements and eliminate overheads associated with user page pinning and translation. It also describes the high-level process of using these buffer rings in FUSE operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer ring and zero-copy capabilities to the FUSE file system over io-uring, reducing memory overhead and enabling direct kernel-to-userspace operations. The changes include using registered memory regions for headers, acquiring folio references for pages, and implementing incremental buffer consumption. Benchmarks show a 20-25% increase in throughput for read-heavy operations with zero-copy enabled. The patch series has dependencies on io-uring kernel-managed buffer rings and registered bvec buffers changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/7] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the list_lru locking mechanism to remove unnecessary NULL checks in callers of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). The changes are based on the understanding that acquiring the lock will not fail, and the function should always return a valid pointer. The patches also include Reviewed-by tags from David Hildenbrand and Lorenzo Stoakes.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch as correct and added an Acked-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:19PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> skip_empty is only for the shrinker to abort and skip a list that's
> empty or whose cgroup is being deleted.
> 
> For list additions and deletions, the cgroup hierarchy is walked
> upwards until a valid list_lru head is found, or it will fall back to
> the node list. Acquiring the lock won't fail. Remove the NULL checks
> in those callers.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.summary: Confirmed that the patch is correct and added a Reviewed-by tag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function, which is currently implemented twice in the mm/list_lru.c file for MEMCG and non-MEMCG variants. The patch consolidates these implementations into a single common function.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the list_lru code to improve its behavior in multi-CPU environments and reduce lock contention. The first patch moves a dead check from the lock_list_lru function to its caller, allowing for unification of the lock_list_lru variants.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary:
Gave Acked-by

Reviewer Shakeel Butt suggested removing the irq parameter from __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags instead, which would allow reclaim code paths to avoid the additional cost of irqsave.
review_comments.raw_body:
If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
would matter here.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that rewriting the workingset shrinker's isolation function to use caller-locked variants would be non-trivial and instead opted for a bool flag, citing maintenance burden.
sentiment_signals: trade-off between code complexity and readability
review_comments.raw_body:
The workingset shrinker's isolation function uses unlock_irq() and
cond_resched(). That would be non-trivial to rewrite - pass flags
around; keep irqs disabled for the whole reclaim cycle; break it into
a two-stage process. This sounds like a higher maintenance burden than
the bool here.

I know there is some cost to this distinction, but I actually do find
it useful to know the difference. It's self-documenting context.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc`, which allocates list_lru heads for folio objects under the control of memory cgroups (memcgs). The function is designed to be lightweight and RCU-safe, allowing it to quickly check if the list_lru heads already exist for a given memcg. If they do not exist, it allocates them. This patch is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru, which requires this helper function to manage folio objects correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) pointed out that the patch does not address the issue of lock ordering violations between the per-vswap spinlock and the folio lock in vswap_free(), which can occur when reclaiming pages from a cgroup. He noted that this is a critical problem that needs to be fixed before the patch can be merged.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/18/26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---

Was just hitting sent on a reply on v2 when I spotted this in my inbox.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split queue's behavior is suboptimal, as it walks the same memcg->split_queue regardless of node, leading to unnecessary splitting of large pages on other nodes. They suggested using list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and requested the introduction of a getter for the deferred_split_lru or making it static in mm/huge_memory.c.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the deferred split queue's use of a spinlock for synchronization can lead to lock ordering issues when reclaiming pages, and suggested using a more robust locking mechanism such as RCU or a per-memcg lock.

review_comments.author: Mikhail Zaslonko
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mikhail Zaslonko reported a reproducible panic on s390 in the dump kernel when running NVMe standalone dump (ngdump), which only occurs during 'capture kernel' boot and not normal boot, indicating a potential issue with the list_lru per-memcg heads instantiation.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Lorenzo's concerns about the deferred split queue's handling of cgroups and its interaction with other shrinkers. The author acknowledged that the current implementation is suboptimal, particularly in how it sets up per-memcg queues and handles node-awareness. They explained that the list_lru API was initially designed for slab objects but has since been extended to allow explicit memcg and NUMA node selection. The author also clarified the callside locking of list_lru structures. Lorenzo's understanding of the current implementation is correct, and the author agreed that it needs improvement.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Lorenzo,

Thanks for taking an in-depth look at this!

Apologies for the late reply, I was traveling last week.

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 01:48:06PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> > queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> > on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> > can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> >
> > 	alloc/unmap
> > 	  deferred_split_folio()
> > 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> > 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> So here it's:
> 
> __do_huge_pmd_anonymous_page() / do_huge_zero_wp_pmd()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_pf()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_nopf()
> -> deferred_split_folio()
> -> set_shrinker_bit()
> 
> Yeah it makes sense to make the first bit succinct anyway :)

Yep I was trying to keep the focus on those parts that interact with
the other stacks listed below. Not biblically accurate
deferred_split_folio() callsites ;)

> > 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> > 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> > 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> > 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> > 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> Hmm there's no such function is this kind of pseudocode, essentially?
> 
> Wouldn't it be clearer to reference:
> 
> shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects ->
> deferred_split_scan()?
> 
> Though I get that it adds verbosity :)
> 
> Sorry not (just) being pedantic here, also so I can understand myself :)

Yes, it's pseudocode. Direct reclaim (shrink_zones) and kswapd
(wake_all_kswapds) do the "for all nodes" loops. Nested within them
are the loops that iterate the cgroup tree (shrink_node_memcgs,
shrink_many, depending on LRU vs MGLRU). And then nested within
*those* we have the shrink_slab() calls.

Again, I tried to keep the boiler plate low and to the point. The most
important thing is: 1) the shrinker bit gets set for each node, memcg
pair that has objects, 2) shrinker visits all nodes x memcgs with it
set 3) but the shrinker scans happen on per-memcg queue, not node-aware.

Let me know if that works as is, or if you would like me to rephrase
that. I would like to keep it as simple as possible, but no simpler
than that ;)

> > 	          deferred_split_scan()
> > 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> Ok so overall there's a per-memcg memcg->split_queue, but we set a bit in
> memcg->nodeinfo[nid]->shrinker_info->unit[blah]->map for it, and when we
> enter shrink_slab_memcg(), we figure out the shrink_control from the
> for_each_set_bit() across memcg->...->unit->map?
> 
> > The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> > has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> > by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> So at this point, regardless of node, we are invoking deferred_split_scan()
> and it's the same memcg->split_queue we are walking, regardless of node?
> 
> Do let me know if my understanding is correct here!
> 
> Hmm that does sound sub-optimal.

That is exactly what's happening. We have all this dance to be precise
about the shrinker bit, but the queue itself is not node-aware.

That makes for some pretty erratic behavior.

> > list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> > streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> > widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> > queue as well.
> 
> It's odd that it wasn't used before?

The list_lru API was initially explicitly for *slab objects*, doing
virt_to_page() and mem_cgroup_from_slab_obj() on the passed in list
heads to derive the proper queue. It was only extended more recently
in 0a97c01cd20b ("list_lru: allow explicit memcg and NUMA node
selection") to allow passing in the queue routing information and
embedding the list heads in other things (such as folios).

The only missing piece now was the support for callside locking of the
list_lru structures.

> >  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   6 +-
> >  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
> >  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
> >  mm/huge_memory.c           | 342 ++++++++++++-------------------------

> It might be nice for the sake of avoiding a global to instead expose this

> references this uses folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(), do something like:

> in mm/huge_memory.c:

> 		return err;
> 	}
> 
> 	return 0;
> }
> 
> And then the callers can just invoke this, and you can make
> deferred_split_lru static in mm/huge_memory.c?

That sounds reasonable. Let me make this change.

It doesn't bother me personally, but I wouldn't object to it being
moved to its own function and have the caller do necessary cleanups.

But yeah sounds like an unrelated refactor :)

> >  	deferred_split_shrinker->count_objects = deferred_split_count;
> >  	deferred_split_shrinker->scan_objects = deferred_split_scan;
> >  	shrinker_register(deferred_split_shrinker);

> >  		shrinker_free(deferred_split_shrinker);
> 
> Presumably no probably-impossible-in-reality race on somebody entering the
> shrinker and referencing the deferred_split_lru before the shrinker is freed?

Ah right, I think for clarity it would indeed be better to destroy the
shrinker, then the queue. Let me re-order this one.

But yes, in practice, none of the above fails. If we have trouble
doing a couple of small kmallocs during a subsys_initcall(), that
machine is unlikely to finish booting, let alone allocate enough
memory to enter the THP shrinker.

> > @@ -953,6 +964,7 @@ static int __init thp_shrinker_init(void)

... and this one as well.

Yeah I stuck with the list_lru internal naming, which uses `lru` for
the struct list_lru, and `l` for struct list_lru_one. I suppose that
was fine for the very domain-specific code and short functions in
there, but it's grating in large, general MM functions like these.

Since `lru` is taken, any preferences? llo?

This is the type of folios that can be queued through
deferred_split_folio(). The order check is inside that function
itself; and the function is only called for anon pages.

File pages are split differently, they don't use the deferred split
shrinker and we don't allocate list_lru heads for them. However, they
still come through this split path here, and so I need to gate whether
it's safe to do list_lru_lock(), __list_lru_del() etc.

> 		}

> 
> static void execute_deferred_dequeue(...) { ... }
> 
> With this implemented either way you'd be able to get rid of the else block
> too.
> 
> obviously only valid if you are able to do the freezing earlier?

The reason it locks the list_lru first is because there can be a race
between the shrinker and a synchronous split attempt.

This is what that comment above it is about:

	/* Prevent deferred_split_scan() touching ->_refcount */

If the shrinker fails to "get" the folio while holding the shrinker
lock, it thinks it beat the free path to the list_lru lock and will
feel responsible for cleaning up the deferred split state:

/* caller holds the list_lru lock already */
static enum lru_status deferred_split_isolate(struct list_head *item,
					      struct list_lru_one *lru,
					      void *cb_arg)
{
	struct folio *folio = container_of(item, struct folio, _deferred_list);
	struct list_head *freeable = cb_arg;

	if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
		list_lru_isolate_move(lru, item, freeable);
		return LRU_REMOVED;
	}

	/* We lost race with folio_put() */
	list_lru_isolate(lru, item);
	if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
		folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
		mod_mthp_stat(folio_order(folio),
			      MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
	}
	return LRU_REMOVED;
}

So if the split path freezes before acquiring the list_lru lock, we
get them both stepping on the deferred split state.

This may or may not be safe given the current manifestation of
list_lru_del() and the folio_test_partially_mapped() tests. But it
feels pretty hairy to let them race and rely on individual tests into
what should be a coherent aggregate state.

> > +	}
> >  	if (folio_ref_freeze(folio, folio_cache_ref_count(folio) + 1)) {
> >  		struct swap_cluster_info *ci = NULL;
> >  		struct lruvec *lruvec;
> >

list head is always safe. Now I need to be explicit to determine if
it's safe to call __list_lru_del() which does all these list_lru
references, which aren't allocated for file.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the reproducible panic on s390 in the dump kernel when running NVMe standalone dump (ngdump). He stated that he would be surprised if it mattered and suggested considering it from a code clarity point of view, implying that the issue is not critical but rather an aesthetic one. No fix or revision plan was mentioned.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 03:51:07PM +0800, Kairui Song wrote:
> On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 4:05\u202fAM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:
> > @@ -4651,13 +4651,19 @@ static struct folio *alloc_swap_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
> >         while (orders) {
> >                 addr = ALIGN_DOWN(vmf->address, PAGE_SIZE << order);
> >                 folio = vma_alloc_folio(gfp, order, vma, addr);
> > -               if (folio) {
> > -                       if (!mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, vma->vm_mm,
> > -                                                           gfp, entry))
> > -                               return folio;
> > +               if (!folio)
> > +                       goto next;
> > +               if (mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, vma->vm_mm, gfp, entry)) {
> >                         count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_SWPIN_FALLBACK_CHARGE);
> >                         folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto next;
> >                 }
> > +               if (folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, gfp)) {
> > +                       folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto fallback;
> > +               }
> 
> Hi Johannes,
> 
> Haven't checked every detail yet, but one question here, might be
> trivial, will it be better if we fallback to the next order instead of
> fallback to 0 order directly? Suppose this is a 2M allocation and 1M
> fallback is allowed, releasing that folio and fallback to 1M will free
> 1M memory which would be enough for the list lru metadata to be
> allocated.

I would be surprised if that mattered. If we can get a 2M folio but
fail a couple of small slab requests, there is probably such extreme
levels of concurrency and pressure on the freelists that the fault has
a good chance of failing altogether and OOMing.

And if it doesn't matter, then let's consider it from a code clarity
point of view. For folio allocation and charging, we reduce the size
to try again. But the list_lru allocation is always the same size - it
would look weird to just try again on failure. If we do so based on
the logic you lay out above, now it needs a comment too...

---

Hello Mikhail,

> This only happens in the 'capture kernel', normal boot of the same kernel works fine.

Thanks for the report. Would you be able to correlate the function
offset in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() to C code? It would be very useful
to understand *which* pointer or data structure it's tripping over.

thp_shrinker_init() is called before start_stop_khugepaged(), so I am
not sure how we could have invalid/uninitialized list_lru structures.

Could you extract the instruction stream sequence surrounding the
crash? I don't see a Code: line, but maybe you can correlate it with
objdump? And then correlate it with C code (make mm/list_lru.lst e.g.)

That would be super helpful. Sorry about the breakage.

Can you verify whether the kdump kernel boots with
cgroup_disable=memory?

I think there is an issue with how we call __list_lru_init(). The
existing callsites had their own memcg_kmem_online() guards. But the
THP one does not, so we're creating a memcg-aware list_lru, but the
do-while hierarchy walk in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() runs into a NULL
memcg.

Can you try the below on top of that -next checkout?

diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
index 1ccdd45b1d14..7c7024e33653 100644
--- a/mm/list_lru.c
+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
@@ -637,7 +637,7 @@ int __list_lru_init(struct list_lru *lru, bool memcg_aware, struct shrinker *shr
 	else
 		lru->shrinker_id = -1;
 
-	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
+	if (mem_cgroup_disabled() || mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
 		memcg_aware = false;
 #endif

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Shakeel Butt noted that the irqsave parameter in lock_list_lru() can be removed if __list_lru_walk_one() is modified to use irq_flags instead, as it's reclaim code path and additional cost of irqsave wouldn't matter here.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:19PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> skip_empty is only for the shrinker to abort and skip a list that's
> empty or whose cgroup is being deleted.
> 
> For list additions and deletions, the cgroup hierarchy is walked
> upwards until a valid list_lru head is found, or it will fall back to
> the node list. Acquiring the lock won't fail. Remove the NULL checks
> in those callers.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:21PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> Only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru() needs to check if there is a
> race with cgroup deletion and list reparenting. Move the check to the
> caller, so that the next patch can unify the lock_list_lru() variants.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:22PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:23PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> Locking is currently internal to the list_lru API. However, a caller
> might want to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state.
> 
> For example, the THP shrinker uses the lock of its custom LRU to keep
> PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent.
> 
> To allow the THP shrinker to switch to list_lru, provide normal and
> irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the
> addition and deletion functions.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

One nit below, other than that:

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

>  
> -static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
> +static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq,
> +				 unsigned long *irq_flags)
>  {
> -	if (irq)
> +	if (irq_flags)
> +		spin_lock_irqsave(&l->lock, *irq_flags);
> +	else if (irq)

If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
would matter here.

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:24PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> memcg_list_lru_alloc() is called every time an object that may end up
> on the list_lru is created. It needs to quickly check if the list_lru
> heads for the memcg already exist, and allocate them when they don't.
> 
> Doing this with folio objects is tricky: folio_memcg() is not stable
> and requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. But it's
> desirable to make the existence check lightweight under RCU, and only
> pin the memcg when we need to allocate list_lru heads and may block.
> 
> In preparation for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru, add a
> helper function for allocating list_lru heads coming from a folio.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:24PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> memcg_list_lru_alloc() is called every time an object that may end up
> on the list_lru is created. It needs to quickly check if the list_lru
> heads for the memcg already exist, and allocate them when they don't.
> 
> Doing this with folio objects is tricky: folio_memcg() is not stable
> and requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. But it's
> desirable to make the existence check lightweight under RCU, and only
> pin the memcg when we need to allocate list_lru heads and may block.
> 
> In preparation for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru, add a
> helper function for allocating list_lru heads coming from a folio.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it's per-NUMA node or per-cgroup rather than their intersection, which can lead to large pages being split on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. He suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists, streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used widely by other major shrinkers.

> TL;DR - anyway, I think get_maintainer.pl --nogit (and trim anyone who
> isn't listed as a maintainer or reviewer) is the right balance to take -
> maintainers with screwed up email set ups (*cough* naming no names
> beginning with L and ending with orenzo) and reviewers who have signed up
> for lots of mail I think are totally fine to always send to.

On some simplistic changes (e.g., simple function rename) I sometimes
drop the Reviewers of subsystems because the simplistic changes are
mostly just as information to maintainers that there might be conflicts.

That helped in the past to reduce the CC lists on series that touch many
archs / subsystems.

-- 
Cheers,

David

flags (trying to get away from system word size flags) - to a trillion
people.

TL;DR - anyway, I think get_maintainer.pl --nogit (and trim anyone who
isn't listed as a maintainer or reviewer) is the right balance to take -
maintainers with screwed up email set ups (*cough* naming no names
beginning with L and ending with orenzo) and reviewers who have signed up
for lots of mail I think are totally fine to always send to.

And of course +cc anybody else you feel appropriate.

The 'git' maintainers entries can absolutely be dropped though, unless
you're explicitly say radically changing something somebody contributed and
it makes sense to include them though all that's subjective!

Cheers, Lorenzo

[0]:https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/cover.1773846935.git.ljs@kernel.org/

> in those callers.

review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() function does not have a kdoc comment, which is requested to be added before adding the Reviewed-by tag.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection, leading to splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. They suggested referencing shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects -> deferred_split_scan() for clarity and proposed exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or making it static in mm/huge_memory.c.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the CC list was not comprehensive and promised to include all relevant maintainers in future patches, but explained that the current patch series was a trimmed down version based on previous discussions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 09:00:57PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> Hi Joannes,
> 
> This is at v3 and you've not cc'd the majority of THP people:
> 
> MEMORY MANAGEMENT - THP (TRANSPARENT HUGE PAGE)
> M:	Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
> M:	David Hildenbrand <david@kernel.org>
> M:	Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>            <--- (!)
> R:	Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
> R:	Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com> <---
> R:	Liam R. Howlett <Liam.Howlett@oracle.com>
> R:	Nico Pache <npache@redhat.com>              <---
> R:	Ryan Roberts <ryan.roberts@arm.com>         <---
> R:	Dev Jain <dev.jain@arm.com>                 <---
> R:	Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>              <---
> R:	Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>           <---
> 
> I'd assume it was an oversight but you're an mm sub-maintainer yourself so
> I'm a little surprised...
> 
> Anyway could you please make sure to do that going forward?
> 
> I'd like to rely on lei's file-tracking stuff to catch cases like this, but
> I just haven't got new mail tracking working properly in that environment
> and it's really too slow.
> 
> It sucks to rely on people cc-ing but email development is what it is :)
> 
> Thanks, Lorenzo

I apologize, there was no ill intent here at all. get_maintainers.pl
gave me 27 addresses for this series and I honestly thought that was
too aggrandizing and self-important for what these patches are lol.

So what you're looking at is a (poor) attempt at trimming down the CC
list based on who I had talked to about these patches at the THP
meeting and who I remember had worked on shrinkers & cgroups.

I can CC everybody going forward, not a problem. Personally I don't
mind getting CCd generously, but my understanding is that some people
do...

---

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 01:51:04PM -0700, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:23PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > Locking is currently internal to the list_lru API. However, a caller
> > might want to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state.
> > 
> > For example, the THP shrinker uses the lock of its custom LRU to keep
> > PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent.
> > 
> > To allow the THP shrinker to switch to list_lru, provide normal and
> > irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the
> > addition and deletion functions.
> > 
> > Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> 
> One nit below, other than that:
> 
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> 
> >  
> > -static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
> > +static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq,
> > +				 unsigned long *irq_flags)
> >  {
> > -	if (irq)
> > +	if (irq_flags)
> > +		spin_lock_irqsave(&l->lock, *irq_flags);
> > +	else if (irq)
> 
> If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
> param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
> would matter here.

The workingset shrinker's isolation function uses unlock_irq() and
cond_resched(). That would be non-trivial to rewrite - pass flags
around; keep irqs disabled for the whole reclaim cycle; break it into
a two-stage process. This sounds like a higher maintenance burden than
the bool here.

I know there is some cost to this distinction, but I actually do find
it useful to know the difference. It's self-documenting context.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Lorenzo's concern about the deferred split queue handling cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, specifically that it's not node-aware and leads to erratic behavior when large pages are split across nodes. The author explained that list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines reclaim walks, and confirmed that converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru would address these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Lorenzo,

Thanks for taking an in-depth look at this!

Apologies for the late reply, I was traveling last week.

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 01:48:06PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> > queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> > on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> > can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> >
> > 	alloc/unmap
> > 	  deferred_split_folio()
> > 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> > 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> So here it's:
> 
> __do_huge_pmd_anonymous_page() / do_huge_zero_wp_pmd()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_pf()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_nopf()
> -> deferred_split_folio()
> -> set_shrinker_bit()
> 
> Yeah it makes sense to make the first bit succinct anyway :)

Yep I was trying to keep the focus on those parts that interact with
the other stacks listed below. Not biblically accurate
deferred_split_folio() callsites ;)

> > 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> > 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> > 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> > 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> > 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> Hmm there's no such function is this kind of pseudocode, essentially?
> 
> Wouldn't it be clearer to reference:
> 
> shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects ->
> deferred_split_scan()?
> 
> Though I get that it adds verbosity :)
> 
> Sorry not (just) being pedantic here, also so I can understand myself :)

Yes, it's pseudocode. Direct reclaim (shrink_zones) and kswapd
(wake_all_kswapds) do the "for all nodes" loops. Nested within them
are the loops that iterate the cgroup tree (shrink_node_memcgs,
shrink_many, depending on LRU vs MGLRU). And then nested within
*those* we have the shrink_slab() calls.

Again, I tried to keep the boiler plate low and to the point. The most
important thing is: 1) the shrinker bit gets set for each node, memcg
pair that has objects, 2) shrinker visits all nodes x memcgs with it
set 3) but the shrinker scans happen on per-memcg queue, not node-aware.

Let me know if that works as is, or if you would like me to rephrase
that. I would like to keep it as simple as possible, but no simpler
than that ;)

> > 	          deferred_split_scan()
> > 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> Ok so overall there's a per-memcg memcg->split_queue, but we set a bit in
> memcg->nodeinfo[nid]->shrinker_info->unit[blah]->map for it, and when we
> enter shrink_slab_memcg(), we figure out the shrink_control from the
> for_each_set_bit() across memcg->...->unit->map?
> 
> > The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> > has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> > by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> So at this point, regardless of node, we are invoking deferred_split_scan()
> and it's the same memcg->split_queue we are walking, regardless of node?
> 
> Do let me know if my understanding is correct here!
> 
> Hmm that does sound sub-optimal.

That is exactly what's happening. We have all this dance to be precise
about the shrinker bit, but the queue itself is not node-aware.

That makes for some pretty erratic behavior.

> > list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> > streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> > widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> > queue as well.
> 
> It's odd that it wasn't used before?

The list_lru API was initially explicitly for *slab objects*, doing
virt_to_page() and mem_cgroup_from_slab_obj() on the passed in list
heads to derive the proper queue. It was only extended more recently
in 0a97c01cd20b ("list_lru: allow explicit memcg and NUMA node
selection") to allow passing in the queue routing information and
embedding the list heads in other things (such as folios).

The only missing piece now was the support for callside locking of the
list_lru structures.

That sounds reasonable. Let me make this change.

The reason it locks the list_lru first is because there can be a race
between the shrinker and a synchronous split attempt.

This is what that comment above it is about:

	/* Prevent deferred_split_scan() touching ->_refcount */

If the shrinker fails to "get" the folio while holding the shrinker
lock, it thinks it beat the free path to the list_lru lock and will
feel responsible for cleaning up the deferred split state:

/* caller holds the list_lru lock already */
static enum lru_status deferred_split_isolate(struct list_head *item,
					      struct list_lru_one *lru,
					      void *cb_arg)
{
	struct folio *folio = container_of(item, struct folio, _deferred_list);
	struct list_head *freeable = cb_arg;

	if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
		list_lru_isolate_move(lru, item, freeable);
		return LRU_REMOVED;
	}

	/* We lost race with folio_put() */
	list_lru_isolate(lru, item);
	if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
		folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
		mod_mthp_stat(folio_order(folio),
			      MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
	}
	return LRU_REMOVED;
}

So if the split path freezes before acquiring the list_lru lock, we
get them both stepping on the deferred split state.

This may or may not be safe given the current manifestation of
list_lru_del() and the folio_test_partially_mapped() tests. But it
feels pretty hairy to let them race and rely on individual tests into
what should be a coherent aggregate state.

> > +	}

> >  		struct swap_cluster_info *ci = NULL;
> >  		struct lruvec *lruvec;
> >

it's safe to call __list_lru_del() which does all these list_lru

> > +		if (dequeue_deferred) {
> > +			__list_lru_del(&deferred_split_lru, l,
> > +				       &folio->_deferred_list, folio_nid(folio));
> >  			if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
> >  				folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
> >  				mod_mthp_stat(old_order,
> >  					MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
> >  			}

And honestly, I think it's nicer to be explicit here about the
expectations of which pages get which treatment.

Filtering file pages on !list_empty() was not very obvious.

> > +	int nid = folio_nid(folio);
> >  	unsigned long flags;
> >  	bool unqueued = false;
> >
> >  	WARN_ON_ONCE(folio_ref_count(folio));
> >  	WARN_ON_ONCE(!mem_cgroup_disabled() && !folio_memcg_charged(folio));
> >
> > -	ds_queue = folio_split_queue_lock_irqsave(folio, &flags);
> > -	if (!list_empty(&folio->_deferred_list)) {
> > -		ds_queue->split_queue_len--;

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the behavior of folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() when allocating list_lru heads for THPs, agreeing that it's not necessary to try again with a smaller size and explaining that it would look weird from a code clarity perspective. The author also acknowledged a request for kdoc comments on two functions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 03:51:07PM +0800, Kairui Song wrote:
> On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 4:05\u202fAM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:
> > @@ -4651,13 +4651,19 @@ static struct folio *alloc_swap_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
> >         while (orders) {
> >                 addr = ALIGN_DOWN(vmf->address, PAGE_SIZE << order);
> >                 folio = vma_alloc_folio(gfp, order, vma, addr);
> > -               if (folio) {
> > -                       if (!mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, vma->vm_mm,
> > -                                                           gfp, entry))
> > -                               return folio;
> > +               if (!folio)
> > +                       goto next;
> > +               if (mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, vma->vm_mm, gfp, entry)) {
> >                         count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_SWPIN_FALLBACK_CHARGE);
> >                         folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto next;
> >                 }
> > +               if (folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, gfp)) {
> > +                       folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto fallback;
> > +               }
> 
> Hi Johannes,
> 
> Haven't checked every detail yet, but one question here, might be
> trivial, will it be better if we fallback to the next order instead of
> fallback to 0 order directly? Suppose this is a 2M allocation and 1M
> fallback is allowed, releasing that folio and fallback to 1M will free
> 1M memory which would be enough for the list lru metadata to be
> allocated.

I would be surprised if that mattered. If we can get a 2M folio but
fail a couple of small slab requests, there is probably such extreme
levels of concurrency and pressure on the freelists that the fault has
a good chance of failing altogether and OOMing.

And if it doesn't matter, then let's consider it from a code clarity
point of view. For folio allocation and charging, we reduce the size
to try again. But the list_lru allocation is always the same size - it
would look weird to just try again on failure. If we do so based on
the logic you lay out above, now it needs a comment too...

---

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 12:01:55PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:24PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > memcg_list_lru_alloc() is called every time an object that may end up
> > on the list_lru is created. It needs to quickly check if the list_lru
> > heads for the memcg already exist, and allocate them when they don't.
> >
> > Doing this with folio objects is tricky: folio_memcg() is not stable
> > and requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. But it's
> > desirable to make the existence check lightweight under RCU, and only
> > pin the memcg when we need to allocate list_lru heads and may block.
> >
> > In preparation for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru, add a
> > helper function for allocating list_lru heads coming from a folio.
> >
> > Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> 
> Logic LGTM, but would be nice to have some kdoc. With that addressed, feel free
> to add:
> 
> Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

Thanks!

> > +#ifdef CONFIG_MEMCG

> > +			       gfp_t gfp);
> 
> Could we have a kdoc comment for this? Thanks!

And one kdoc comment.

Your total will be 8.75, you can pull up to the next window, sir.

Thanks for the report. Would you be able to correlate the function
offset in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() to C code? It would be very useful
to understand *which* pointer or data structure it's tripping over.

thp_shrinker_init() is called before start_stop_khugepaged(), so I am
not sure how we could have invalid/uninitialized list_lru structures.

Could you extract the instruction stream sequence surrounding the
crash? I don't see a Code: line, but maybe you can correlate it with
objdump? And then correlate it with C code (make mm/list_lru.lst e.g.)

That would be super helpful. Sorry about the breakage.

Can you verify whether the kdump kernel boots with
cgroup_disable=memory?

I think there is an issue with how we call __list_lru_init(). The
existing callsites had their own memcg_kmem_online() guards. But the
THP one does not, so we're creating a memcg-aware list_lru, but the
do-while hierarchy walk in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() runs into a NULL
memcg.

Can you try the below on top of that -next checkout?

diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
index 1ccdd45b1d14..7c7024e33653 100644
--- a/mm/list_lru.c
+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
@@ -637,7 +637,7 @@ int __list_lru_init(struct list_lru *lru, bool memcg_aware, struct shrinker *shr
 	else
 		lru->shrinker_id = -1;
 
-	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
+	if (mem_cgroup_disabled() || mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
 		memcg_aware = false;
 #endif

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup but not their intersection, leading to potential issues when splitting large pages on other nodes during reclaim. They suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists, streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers.

review_comments.author: Mikhail Zaslonko
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mikhail Zaslonko reported a reproducible panic on s390 in the dump kernel when running NVMe standalone dump (ngdump), which only happens in the 'capture kernel', normal boot of the same kernel works fine, and requested further investigation.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 18-Mar-26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. 
> The queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That 
> means on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering 
> reclaim can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> alloc/unmap deferred_split_folio() list_add_tail(memcg-
> >split_queue) set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes) mem_cgroup_iter() 
> shrink_slab(node, memcg) shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg) if 
> test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id) 
> deferred_split_scan() walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the
> cgroup has a single large page on the node of interest, all large
> pages owned by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be
> split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus,
> it streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's 
> used widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred 
> split queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the 
> first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling 
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages 
> are created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, 
> so the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> --- include/ 
> linux/huge_mm.h    |   6 +- include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 - 
> include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 -- mm/huge_memory.c           | 342 
> ++++++++++++------------------------- mm/internal.h              | 2
> +- mm/khugepaged.c            |   7 + mm/memcontrol.c |  12 +- mm/
> memory.c                |  52 +++--- mm/ mm_init.c               |
> 15 -- 9 files changed, 151 insertions(+), 301 deletions(-)
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] x86/tdx: Port I/O emulation fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an off-by-one error in the port I/O handling code for x86/tdx architecture, specifically in the handle_in() and handle_out() functions of arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c. The issue arises from the GENMASK macro being used with a size parameter that is one bit too large, resulting in an incorrect mask calculation. The patch corrects this by subtracting 1 from the size parameter.

review_comments.author: Kuppuswamy Sathyanarayanan
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and suggested including a link to the bug report in the commit log.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Kirill,

On 3/31/2026 4:24 AM, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> handle_in() and handle_out() in arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c use:
> 
>     u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size, 0);
> 
> GENMASK(h, l) includes bit h. For size=1 (INB), this produces
> GENMASK(8, 0) = 0x1FF (9 bits) instead of GENMASK(7, 0) = 0xFF (8
> bits). The mask is one bit too wide for all I/O sizes.
> 
> Fix the mask calculation.
> 
> Fixes: 03149948832a ("x86/tdx: Port I/O: Add runtime hypercalls")
> Reported-by: Borys Tsyrulnikov <tsyrulnikov.borys@gmail.com>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> ---

LGTM. Can you include a link to the bug report or related discussion in 
the commit log? It will help understand the impact of this issue.

Reviewed-by: Kuppuswamy Sathyanarayanan <sathyanarayanan.kuppuswamy@linux.intel.com>

>  arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c | 4 ++--
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c b/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
> index 7b2833705d47..4d7f71d50122 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
> +++ b/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
> @@ -693,7 +693,7 @@ static bool handle_in(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
>  		.r13 = PORT_READ,
>  		.r14 = port,
>  	};
> -	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size, 0);
> +	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size - 1, 0);
>  	bool success;
>  
>  	/*
> @@ -713,7 +713,7 @@ static bool handle_in(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
>  
>  static bool handle_out(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
>  {
> -	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size, 0);
> +	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size - 1, 0);
>  
>  	/*
>  	 * Emulate the I/O write via hypercall. More info about ABI can be found

-- 
Sathyanarayanan Kuppuswamy
Linux Kernel Developer
